era_context: Classical Greece in the early Classical period, dominated by the aftermath of the Persian Wars (480–479 BCE). Athens under Cimon and the early Delian League; Sparta consolidating Peloponnesian hegemony. Egypt under Persian rule (Achaemenid Empire); China in the Spring and Autumn period transitioning toward the Warring States era. This is the age of early Greek philosophy, temple construction, and the consolidation of democratic institutions in Athens.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (sparse contemporary records)
Mesoamerica (no written records in this period)

events.title: Pericles rises to prominence in Athens; democratic reforms accelerate
events.description: Pericles, backed by popular support and the ostracism of his rival Cimon (461 BCE), emerges as the leading statesman of Athens. He champions radical democratic reforms, including payment for jury service and expansion of the citizen assembly's powers. This marks the beginning of the Periclean Age, which will define Athenian cultural and political dominance for the next three decades. The shift consolidates power in the hands of the demos (common people) rather than the aristocracy.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (retrospective account)

events.certainty_note: Pericles' rise is well-attested in later sources, but precise dating of his ascendancy varies (461–460 BCE range). Cimon's ostracism is dated to 461 BCE by most sources; Pericles' prominence solidifies in 460 BCE.

events.title: Egyptian revolt against Persian rule; Inarus leads uprising
events.description: Inarus, a Libyan prince, leads a major rebellion against Achaemenid Persian rule in Egypt. He gains control of much of the Delta and Lower Egypt, declaring himself pharaoh. The revolt is initially successful, with Persian forces under Artaxerxes I struggling to suppress it. Inarus seeks alliance with Athens, which sends a naval expedition to support the rebellion. This conflict will dominate Egyptian politics for the next several years and represents a significant challenge to Persian imperial authority.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1

events.certainty_note: The rebellion is well-attested in Greek sources, though Egyptian records are sparse. Dating to 460 BCE is conventional; some sources place the outbreak in 461 BCE. The chronology of the revolt is debated among modern scholars.

events.title: Construction of the Temple of Zeus at Olympia begins (or continues)
events.description: The Temple of Zeus at Olympia, one of the greatest Doric temples of Classical Greece, is under construction or in advanced stages of completion. Designed by the architect Libon of Elis, the temple will house the famous chryselephantine (gold and ivory) statue of Zeus by Phidias. The temple represents the height of Classical Greek architectural achievement and religious devotion. Its construction reflects the prosperity and cultural confidence of the Greek city-states in the post-Persian War period.
events.certainty_note: Construction dates for the Temple of Zeus are debated. Most scholars place the main construction phase between 470–456 BCE, with completion around 456 BCE. The temple may have been under construction in 460 BCE, but the exact year is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Pericles' rise to power is sometimes placed at 461 BCE rather than 460 BCE, depending on the source. The Egyptian revolt's exact start date is disputed: some sources suggest 461 BCE. The Temple of Zeus construction chronology is debated among archaeologists; some place the primary building phase slightly later (465–456 BCE). No major disconfirming evidence undermines the significance of these events, but precision in dating should be treated with caution.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 460 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Athenian sources, which creates a Western-centric bias. Egyptian and Persian perspectives are largely lost or filtered through Greek accounts. The Athenian sources (Thucydides, Plutarch) are retrospective, written decades or centuries later, and reflect Athenian political interests. Archaeological evidence from Olympia and Athens provides material corroboration but does not resolve all dating ambiguities. The record is richest for Athens and Greece; sparser for Egypt, Persia, and other regions.

graph_edges.note: Pericles' rise follows directly from Cimon's ostracism in 461 BCE
Egyptian revolt prompts Athenian military intervention
Both reflect the cultural and political confidence of Classical Athens
